one_line_takeaway: FlexGuard replaces binary safe/unsafe LLM moderation with a rubric-distilled, GRPO-aligned continuous 0-100 risk score that can be re-thresholded per deployment, cutting the best-to-worst strictness-regime F1 gap that plagues binary moderators (up to 19.2% for Qwen3Guard) while beating the strongest baseline by 5.85-9.64 F1 points on the paper's own FlexBench benchmark.
key_finding: On FlexBench, existing SOTA moderators show large cross-strictness F1 swings: best-to-worst-regime F1 drop of 19.2% for Qwen3Guard and 15.7% for BingoGuard on prompt moderation, and 14.8% (Qwen3Guard) / 8.9% (GPT-5) on response moderation (Figure 2), even after regime-specific logit-threshold or rubric-conditioned-prompt adaptation. FlexGuard with calibrated thresholding achieves the best average and worst-regime F1 on FlexBench for both prompt moderation (Average 81.78, Worst 78.26) and response moderation (Average 80.29, Worst 75.81) among all evaluated methods, outperforming the strongest competitor by 5.85 points (Doubao-1.8, 77.26 average) on prompt moderation and 9.64 points (GPT-5, 76.90 average) on response moderation, per the paper's own comparison in Table 1. On public benchmarks with original binary labels (Table 2), FlexGuard attains average F1 of 84.02 (prompt: ToxicChat 74.40, WildGuard 87.50, XSTest 92.34, OpenAI 81.14, Aegis2.0 85.44) and 88.32 (response: SafeRLHF 92.92, WildGuard 80.87, HarmBench 91.32, BeaverTails 85.44, XSTest 91.03, Aegis2.0 84.16), competitive with but not uniformly beating GuardReasoner-8B, WildGuard-7B, and BingoGuard-8B, while the paper notes it trains on fewer data sources. Ablations (Table 3) show: Binary-SFT -> Score-SFT with Beta(2,8)/Beta(8,2) soft targets gives limited robustness gain; Score-SFT with LLM-rubric-distilled scores gives a substantial jump (e.g., prompt average 66.96 -> with calibration 77.85); adding GRPO with the full category+score reward gives the largest additional gain (full FlexGuard: prompt avg 81.78/worst 78.26, response avg 80.29/worst 75.81); GRPO trained with category-accuracy reward alone (no score regression) degrades performance (prompt avg 69.69), underscoring the necessity of the score-regression term. Judge-severity monotonicity: Spearman's rho = 0.828 (prompt) / 0.8113 (response), Kendall's tau = 0.7307 (prompt) / 0.7225 (response) between judge 0-100 scores and human 5-tier severity on a 1,000-instance held-out set.
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions are two: (1) FlexBench, a human-annotated benchmark of 4K instances (2K prompts, 2K prompt-response pairs) across seven risk categories (VIO, ILG, SEX, INF, DIS, MIS, JAIL) and five severity tiers (BENIGN, LOW, MODERATE, HIGH, EXTREME), mapped to three enforcement-strictness regimes (strict, moderate, loose), designed to measure whether a moderator's decisions stay reliable as the strictness definition shifts; and (2) FlexGuard, an LLM-based moderator (Qwen3-8B backbone) that outputs a risk category plus a calibrated continuous risk score in [0,100] instead of a binary safe/unsafe label, so that different strictness regimes can be realized by thresholding the same score rather than retraining or reprompting. FlexGuard is trained via a rubric-guided score-distillation pipeline (an LLM judge scores rubric-defined severity, then scores are calibrated to stay consistent with source binary labels) followed by two-stage risk-alignment training: SFT warm-up on distilled (category, score, rationale) targets, then GRPO with a dense reward combining category-accuracy (+1/-1) and score-regression terms.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the traditional security sense; the paper frames the problem as operational robustness of content moderators to a non-adversarial but shifting deployment variable -- 'enforcement strictness' (how conservatively a platform defines/enforces harmfulness), which differs across platforms (e.g., X permits labeled adult content, some Reddit communities do not) and evolves over time. The 'risk' being defended against is a binary moderator being brittle/inconsistent when the operative harmfulness threshold changes, not an adaptive adversary trying to evade the moderator (though the Ethical Considerations section separately flags dual-use/evasion-probing risk as a deployment consideration, not something the method is evaluated against).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) benchmark construction and all experiments are English-only, so severity rubrics and FlexGuard's effectiveness are unvalidated for multilingual/code-mixed settings; (2) the risk-score distillation pipeline relies on only two public training sources (Aegis2.0, WildGuardMix), and the paper does not systematically study how adding other sources or shifting training distribution affects calibration/robustness; (3) the GRPO alignment stage uses a single designed score-regression reward and does not explore alternative post-training algorithms (e.g., DAPO/GSPO-style variants) that might further reduce sensitivity to noisy pseudo-labels. Additional grounded observations from the tables: FlexGuard is not uniformly best -- on public benchmarks it trails GuardReasoner-8B and BingoGuard-8B on several individual datasets (e.g., ToxicChat 74.40 vs GuardReasoner's 74.83/BingoGuard's 76.07; XSTest 92.34 vs 93.33/94.50); LLM-judge/human agreement used for pseudo-label construction tops out at 69.9%/63.2% even for the best judge, meaning training supervision itself has substantial noise; PKU-SafeRLHF-8B has no prompt-moderation results ('/' in Table 1 and Table 2) since it is response-only.

datasets_used: FlexBench (novel, this paper: 4K instances, 2K prompts + 2K prompt-response pairs, 7 risk categories, 5 severity tiers, 3 strictness regimes)
HarmBench (Mazeika et al. 2024 / Zeng et al. 2025 cite mismatch in text)
quantitative_results: FlexBench prompt moderation: FlexGuard calibrated thresholding Average F1 81.78, Worst-regime F1 78.26 (Table 1)
FlexBench response moderation: FlexGuard calibrated thresholding Average F1 80.29, Worst-regime F1 75.81 (Table 1)
FlexGuard outperforms strongest competitor by 5.85 F1 points (vs Doubao-1.8, prompt) and 9.64 F1 points (vs GPT-5, response)
Cross-strictness F1 drop (best to worst regime), prompt moderation: Qwen3Guard -19.2%, BingoGuard -15.7%, GPT-5 -8.5% (Figure 2)
Cross-strictness F1 drop, response moderation: Qwen3Guard -14.8%, GPT-5 -8.9%, BingoGuard -4.5% (Figure 2)
LLM judge-human label agreement: 69.9% (prompt), 63.2% (response) for full annotation workflow
Best LLM judge (Doubao-1.6-Pro) agreement with humans on 1,000-instance held-out set: 72.1% prompt (with calibration), 65.1% response (with calibration) (Table 4)
Judge score-severity monotonicity: Spearman rho 0.828 (prompt), 0.8113 (response); Kendall tau 0.7307 (prompt), 0.7225 (response) (Table 5)
Ablation: Binary-SFT prompt avg 64.31/worst 59.49 vs Full FlexGuard prompt avg 81.78/worst 78.26 (Table 3)
Ablation: GRPO with category-only reward degrades performance to prompt avg 69.69 vs full reward's 81.78
Public benchmarks average F1: FlexGuard 84.02 (prompt), 88.32 (response) vs BingoGuard-8B 84.36/87.18, GuardReasoner-8B 82.82/86.38 (Table 2)
